entries.label: Networking
entries.rationale: Network fetch, protocol policy, and socket behavior remain browser CPU/process responsibilities.

entries.label: Cache
entries.rationale: HTTP/cache storage and invalidation are browser storage responsibilities outside the WebGPU seam.

entries.label: HTML parsing
entries.rationale: Markup parsing is owned by the browser engine and must not move into Doe.

entries.label: CSS parsing
entries.rationale: CSS tokenization and parsing remain browser engine responsibilities.

entries.label: Cascade
entries.rationale: Cascade resolution is browser policy and layout input, not a Doe execution target.

entries.label: DOM
entries.rationale: DOM construction and mutation semantics stay inside the browser engine.

entries.label: Style tree
entries.rationale: Style tree construction is browser-owned input to rendering.

entries.label: Layout
entries.rationale: Layout semantics affect accessibility, hit testing, and web compatibility; Doe may observe downstream GPU work but not own layout.

entries.label: JavaScript execution
entries.rationale: JavaScript execution remains V8/browser-owned and is not a Doe runtime surface.

entries.label: Event loop
entries.rationale: Task scheduling and event dispatch stay in the browser runtime.

entries.label: Accessibility tree
entries.rationale: Accessibility semantics remain browser-owned and cannot depend on Doe runtime behavior.

entries.label: Permissions
entries.rationale: Permission decisions remain Chromium policy; Doe can only consume approved GPU access.

entries.label: Origin policy
entries.rationale: Origin isolation and security policy remain browser responsibilities.

entries.label: Scheduling
entries.rationale: Browser task scheduling stays CPU-owned, while Doe can receipt downstream GPU command timing.

entries.label: Lifecycle
entries.rationale: Page lifecycle remains browser-owned; Doe can observe device loss and runtime teardown artifacts.

entries.label: Workers
entries.rationale: Worker execution is browser-owned, but WebGPU work issued by workers can be captured at the runtime seam.

entries.label: Service workers
entries.rationale: Service worker fetch and lifecycle behavior stays outside Doe.

entries.label: Developer tooling
entries.rationale: Browser developer tools remain CPU-owned, while Doe can expose runtime identity, shader links, cache state, and fallback receipts.

entries.label: Rasterization
entries.rationale: Rasterization is GPU-owned; Doe can observe it only where Chromium exposes stable WebGPU/canvas command boundaries.

entries.label: Compositing
entries.rationale: Compositing is GPU-owned but remains browser-policy constrained outside promoted contracts.

entries.label: Canvas 2D
entries.rationale: Canvas 2D can become observable through fusion probes when stable command boundaries exist.

entries.label: WebGL
entries.rationale: WebGL GPU work is observable in browser GPU lanes but is not a Doe replacement target without a promoted contract.

entries.label: WebGPU
entries.rationale: WebGPU is the promoted Doe browser seam when forced-runtime and hidden-fallback gates pass.

entries.label: Image filters
entries.rationale: Image filters can be observed where the browser exposes GPU command boundaries.

entries.label: CSS effects
entries.rationale: CSS effects may be observable as GPU work but CSS semantics remain browser-owned.

entries.label: Transforms
entries.rationale: Transform execution can be observed in GPU traces when the browser exposes stable command boundaries.

entries.label: Texture upload
entries.rationale: Texture upload is a promoted comparison surface for native runtime and browser workloads when structural equivalence holds.

entries.label: Readback
entries.rationale: Readback can support claims only with matched command graph receipts and timing scope symmetry.

entries.label: Video presentation
entries.rationale: Video presentation touches media policy and shared textures; Doe cannot claim it without a promoted browser-policy contract.

entries.label: Swapchain/surface presentation
entries.rationale: Surface presentation is part of WebGPU/canvas presentation behavior and must be validated by browser-lane artifacts.

entries.label: GPU memory residency
entries.rationale: Doe can receipt resource allocation and lifetime where runtime traces expose the data.

entries.label: Command submission
entries.rationale: Command submission is a Doe runtime comparison surface when both sides measure the same submit/wait scope.

entries.label: Pipeline cache
entries.rationale: Pipeline cache behavior can be claimed only through explicit cold/warm contracts and disclosed cache state.

entries.label: Shader compilation
entries.rationale: Shader compilation is claimable only through Doe-vs-Tint evidence with source/output hashes and validation receipts.

entries.label: Frame pacing
entries.rationale: Frame pacing is observable through browser diagnostics but not claimable until repeated browser claim windows cover it.

boundaries.crossing: JavaScript issues WebGPU API calls through navigator.gpu.
boundaries.rationale: This is the primary Chromium runtime-selection seam.

boundaries.crossing: Worker-originated WebGPU commands enter the same runtime seam.
boundaries.rationale: Doe can observe the command stream after browser policy accepts the worker context.

boundaries.crossing: WGSL sources lower through Doe/Tint compiler paths.
boundaries.rationale: Compiler evidence can link source to IR and backend output for browser shader rows.

boundaries.crossing: Encoded commands enter runtime queue submission and wait scopes.
boundaries.rationale: Submit/wait claims require timing-phase symmetry and command graph receipts.

boundaries.crossing: WebGPU write/copy operations move host data into GPU textures.
boundaries.rationale: Upload claims require explicit path-asymmetry annotations and structural equivalence.

boundaries.crossing: GPU results return to mapped buffers or browser-visible outputs.
boundaries.rationale: Readback claims require matched GPU work and identical readback scope.

boundaries.crossing: Canvas commands reach GPU rasterization or fallback paths.
boundaries.rationale: Canvas/GPU fusion probes can observe output hashes and fallback reasons when command boundaries are stable.

boundaries.crossing: Browser CSS effects lower to GPU filter or compositing work.
boundaries.rationale: Doe can receipt only GPU-visible work; CSS semantics remain browser-owned.

boundaries.crossing: Composited frames reach swapchain or surface presentation.
boundaries.rationale: Presentation behavior is validated by browser-lane smoke and layered artifacts.

boundaries.crossing: External video/image resources may enter GPU textures.
boundaries.rationale: External texture and media import behavior needs a promoted browser-policy contract before claims.

boundaries.crossing: Developer-visible surfaces expose runtime identity, shader links, cache state, and unsupported-capability receipts.
boundaries.rationale: The map permits developer-visible evidence without changing browser semantics.
